Field Notes from a Long Migration

By Lin Okafor · May 12, 2026

We spent the last fourteen months moving a fifteen-year-old monolith to a federated graph without taking the site down. This post is not a retrospective — the migration is not done — but a set of field notes about what hurt and what turned out to be easy. I will keep appending entries as the work continues.

Entry 1 — The first extraction

The temptation when extracting a service is to start with the database schema. Resist it. Start with the reads: pick one well-understood page, trace every query it issues, and write a small shim that serves those reads from a new service backed by a copy of the data. The page is now served from two places at once, which gives you a comparison harness for free.

We did this for the order-status page first. It was the right choice not because it was the most valuable page, but because everyone understood it, the data was bounded, and the failure mode was visible to the support team within minutes. The extraction took two weeks. The next one took four days.

Entry 2 — Propagation, not cutover

A long migration lives or dies by how many concurrent paths you can keep in flight. We never cut over. We propagated: each new service ran dark, receiving duplicated traffic, with its responses compared to the monolith's. Discrepancies were bugs in the new service, not the old one — the old one was the source of truth until it wasn't.

Shadow traffic at scale is expensive, so we sampled: one percent for boring endpoints, one hundred percent for the ones we were actively shaking out. The comparison harness ran inside the monolith, diffing its own response against the shadow response before returning. The cost was latency on the shadow path, which users never see.

Entry 3 — When the graph fights back

The federated graph layer was the last thing we added and the first thing that hurt. Composition failures — one subgraph breaking the supergraph for everyone — turned a deploy into a coordinated dance. The answer was not better tooling, it was a smaller surface: every subgraph now exposes a tiny read-only API, and the composition layer has no business logic of its own.

The lesson, which I keep relearning, is that federation is not an architectural decision so much as an operational one. The shape of the graph matters less than the discipline of changing it.
